questions.clue: In linguistics, this term describes any newly formed word, term, or phrase that has gained sufficient popular or institutional recognition to be entering mainstream language — 'selfie' was once one.

citations.title: Neologism
citations.snippet: In linguistics, a neologism is any newly formed word, term, or phrase that has gained popular or institutional recognition and is becoming accepted into mainstream language.

questions.clue: Named after an Oxford don who reportedly told students they had 'hissed all his mystery lectures' and produced examples like 'blushing crow' for 'crushing blow,' this term describes the accidental transposition of initial consonants between words.

citations.title: Spoonerism
citations.snippet: A spoonerism is an occurrence of speech in which corresponding consonants, vowels, or morphemes are switched between two words of a phrase. These are named after the Oxford don and priest William Archibald Spooner, who reportedly commonly s

questions.clue: This clinical term for a parasomnia describes a state of dissociation in which features of wakefulness and deep non-REM sleep occur simultaneously in different brain regions, causing a person to perform normal activities while technically asleep.

citations.title: Sleepwalking
citations.snippet: Sleepwalking, also known as somnambulism or noctambulism, is a phenomenon of combined sleep and wakefulness. It is classified as a sleep disorder belonging to the parasomnia family. It can also be understood as a disorder of state dissociat

questions.clue: The English village of Eyam famously imposed this public health measure on itself in 1665, voluntarily sealing its borders so the bubonic plague it was suffering from could not spread to neighboring communities.

citations.title: Quarantine
citations.snippet: A quarantine is a restriction on the movement of people, animals, and goods which is intended to prevent the spread of disease or pests. It is often used in connection to disease and illness, preventing the movement of those who may have be

questions.clue: This execution apparatus was designed during the Enlightenment to make capital punishment more reliable and humane; its very name dates from the French Revolution, though similar devices had existed in Europe for centuries before then, and France last used it on September 10, 1977.

citations.title: Guillotine
citations.snippet: A guillotine is an apparatus designed for effectively carrying out executions by beheading. The device consists of a tall, upright frame with a weighted and angled blade suspended at the top. The condemned person is secured with a pillory a

questions.clue: Cutting portrait profiles from black card became popular in the mid-18th century, and this term for such images — now also applied to any dark shape seen against a lighter background — entered common English use in the early 19th century.

citations.title: Silhouette
citations.snippet: A silhouette is the image of a person, animal, object or scene represented as a solid shape of a single colour, usually black, with its edges matching the outline of the subject. The interior of a silhouette is featureless, and the silhouet

questions.clue: In Australian English, this term for a small body of still water — such as an oxbow lake or a river channel that leads to a dead end — is likely borrowed from Wiradjuri, an Aboriginal Australian language of New South Wales.

citations.title: Billabong
citations.snippet: In Australian English, a billabong is a small body of water, usually a permanent one created by a change in course or the flooding of a river. It is variously used to refer to oxbow lakes, dry creek beds that fill after heavy rainfall and c

questions.clue: This Latin phrase, literally meaning 'in good faith,' describes a sincere intention to be fair and honest regardless of outcome, and is an important concept in both contract law and business negotiations.

citations.title: Good faith
citations.snippet: In human interactions, good faith is a sincere intention to be fair, open, and honest, regardless of the outcome of the interaction. It is an important concept within law and business. The opposed concepts are bad faith, mala fides (duplici

questions.clue: This Latin phrase for the existing state of affairs is commonly used in policy debate to contrast current conditions with a proposed change, as in 'maintaining the nuclear arsenals at current levels.'

citations.title: Status quo
citations.snippet: Status quo is a Latin phrase meaning the existing state of affairs, particularly with regard to social, economic, legal, environmental, political, religious, scientific or military issues. In the sociological sense, the status quo refers to

questions.clue: This French phrase — literally 'already seen' — names the uncanny psychological sensation of experiencing a seemingly new situation as deeply familiar, as if one has lived through it before.

citations.title: Déjà vu
citations.snippet: Déjà vu is the phenomenon of feeling like one has lived through the present situation in the past. It is an illusion of memory whereby—despite a strong sense of recollection—the time, place, and context of the "previous" experience are unce
